sections.title: 9 Sim-to-Real Transfer (Final Round)
sections.content: The competition had two rounds. Everything up to this point the policy, the RL pipeline, the inference tuning, the 1st-place online result of Section 8 was the simulation round. The final round was sim-to-real: take policy trained in Isaac Sim and make it fold real garments on real bimanual robot. had roughly three months for the simulation round and bit more than one week for sim-to-real. So the overwhelming majority of the work, experiments, and ideas in this report are about simulation; the sim-to-real part was hackathon-style sprint where tried to get working transfer and fine-tune onto the real robot as quickly as possible, not careful study. This section documents what did and what would do differently. 17 9.1 Why zero-shot didnt work Simulation differs from reality in many aspects: camera sensors and placement, robot mechanics and backlash, and garment physics. The provided simulator is very capable, but the gap was still far too large for zero-shot transfer. am fairly confident large part of the problem was that my policy overfit to sim-specific details. The clearest indirect evidence came from an image-processing test. My model resizes each 640480 camera image to 224224. If, instead of resizing directly, first downsized to 320240 and only then to 224224 change that is nearly invisible to the human eye the simulation success rate dropped significantly. And when looked at the auxiliary-head predictions, the model could perfectly distinguish 640224 image from 640320224 one. This type of overfitting doesnt transfer well to the real robot. Some tasks were also completed differently in the real dataset than in simulation. The short-sleeve top was folded starting from the other sleeve; long pants were grabbed from the top in simulation but from the bottom (relative to the camera) in the real data; and the shorts were rotated 180 degrees in half of the real episodes. In general you would want this kind of generalization when training robot to fold clothes in the real world, but in the competition setting it just introduced unnecessary multimodality that hurt performance in the short run. There was also an extra condition that made this competition harder than ordinary sim-to-real. never had access to the actual robot the final evaluation would run on. So this was really sim my robot their robot sim-to-real-toreal, with an additional generalization step baked in. Even the behavior-cloning dataset the organizers provided had been collected on slightly different physical setup (different lighting, calibration, camera positions) than either my rig or the evaluation rig. fitting illustration of how differently sim and real are perceived: spent two to three months certain we were folding ordinary, adult-sized clothes. It was only when assembled the real follower rig for the final round that realized we had been dealing with kids clothes the entire time. genuinely funny revelation and good reminder that the mental model you build inside simulator can be wrong in ways you never think to check. 9.2 Two ways to close domain gap There are two fundamental levers for closing the gap between two environments: make them more similar, or make the training distribution more diverse so the gap falls inside it. leaned on both, and the rest of this section is organized around them alignment work (matching cameras, calibration, units, motion intensity to the target) and diversity work (heavy augmentation, varied data sources, DAgger). 9.3 Strategy overview Concretely, the sim-to-real recipe was: 1. Start from late but not latest checkpoint of the sim policy (9.4). 2. Strip the model down to the heads and logic that make sense on the real robot (9.5). 3. Fine-tune on three-bucket mix of real organizer data, my own teleop/DAgger data, and sim replays (9.6). 4. Align my setup and the sim as closely as possible to the target setup (9.7). 5. Apply very heavy augmentation during training (9.8). 6. Resample each sources motion intensity so the buckets are consistent (9.9). 7. Collect DAgger data progressively and weight it by intervention proximity (9.10). 9.4 Starting checkpoint transferred from sim checkpoint that was strong but deliberately not the very latest one. The latest sim checkpoints were the most specialized to the simulator (and, per 9.1, the most overfit to its rendering quirks); slightly earlier checkpoint was better-conditioned starting point for fine-tuning onto new domain. 9.5 Stripping the model down to the real task Most of the auxiliary machinery from the simulation policy depends on simulator-only privileged information (keypoint distances, success labels from the checker, advantage estimates) that does not exist on the real robot. For real training disabled all of it and kept only what transfers: Still trained: the action objective, the garment-type head, and the completion head. Garment type is needed for the inference-time bootstrap (7.6); completion is progress signal that trains on any data. Kept but frozen (the head weights exist and load from the sim checkpoint, but get no gradient): success, midtask checkpoint, and time-to-completion. Removed entirely: the keypoint-distance head and both world-model heads (the FAST-conditioned head and the action-conditional head) all of them depend on sim-only targets. Removed from the pipeline: advantage conditioning and the AdaRMS advantage channel (no advantage on real), and therefore classifier-free guidance and best-ofN selection at inference (both need the advantage / machinery). Real inference is the simpler path: denoised chunk, soft inpainting between chunks, and the garment-type bootstrap nothing else. So the real policy is essentially the initial VLM, the action expert, plus two lightweight heads, fine-tuned from model that had learned great deal more in sim. 9.6 The training mix fine-tuned on three buckets of data, with fixed target share of each per training batch. Table 6 lists the buckets, their sources, how much was collected, and the role each plays. The reasoning behind the weights: the organizer dataset is the closest thing had to the actual evaluation distribution, so it dominates; my own data is valuable but collected on mismatched rig, so it is down-weighted on purpose; sim data is there mostly to keep the model from forgetting and to add variety. Because the buckets differ so much in raw size, the fixed batch shares translate into very different per-frame sampling rates the large home_real and home_sim pools are heavily down-sampled relative to primary_real (sampling-rate column of Table 6). In total collected 792 episodes (451k frames) of my own real-robot data on top of the organizers 500; including the BC set, the real training pool is about 1,292 episodes / 638k frames, with further 1,723 sim success-replay episodes (625k frames) in the sim bucket. Figure 11 shows the four physical garment types the real policy folds. The three data sources, in turn, look visibly different from each other different lighting, garment instances, and camera framing which is exactly the differences that we need to deal with (Figure 12). 18 Source Episodes Frames Share Sampling rate Role Organizer BC 500 187,135 60% 1.00 Most aligned with the eval setup (same cameras, robot colors, garments, folding pattern) the canonical target distribution. My home teleop My home DAgger 321 471 162,363 288,198 30% 0.21 Differs from the eval setup, but the only source of recovery. Kept deliberately lower because of that mismatch. Sim success-replays 1,723 625,111 10% 0.05 Already-collected successful sim rollouts, replayed in realformat pipeline with heavy augmentation regularizing mix. Table 6: The three-bucket real training mix: batch share, per-frame sampling rate, episodes, and frames per source. The two home sources (teleop + DAgger) form single bucket and share one batch share, sampling rate, and role. 9.8 Heavy augmentation On top of environment alignment, applied very aggressive training-time augmentation much stronger than in the sim round. The stack includes strong, per-cameraindependent color jitter (the home top-camera is noticeably darker than the eval camera; sim is over-bright), per-channel gain and gamma, blur, additive sensor noise, independent crop/rotate/zoom/translate on every camera (to cover cameramount drift), cutout, random camera dropout, and state noise / dropout that pushes the policy to trust the images over potentially-miscalibrated proprioception. The sim-replay bucket additionally goes through the heavy environmentaugmentation engine from the data-collection round (pattern swap, color remap, camera and arm-base jitter calibrated against the real rig, table-texture and light variation). Figure 15 shows the same frame under several independent draws of the stack. 9.9 Aligning motion intensity across sources The three buckets were recorded at very different speeds, and that matters because the model predicts deltas over fixed horizon: source where the arms move slowly produces small per-step deltas, fast source produces large ones, and mixing them naively teaches the model an inconsistent notion of how far to move in one step. After RL, the sim policy moved quite fast, while my manual teleop and especially the early DAgger corrections were very slow. So each source carries speed factor that resamples its time axis before chunking, rescaling its motion intensity to match the primary_real baseline (Figure 16). factor above 1 compresses more native wall-clock time into one model step (used to speed up slow sources); factor below 1 does the reverse (used to slow down the fast sim): The organizer BC is the baseline (1.0). Home teleop is gentler and runs 1.4 longer per episode, so it is compressed (1.5). Sim rollouts are the most aggressive, so they are stretched (0.65). DAgger corrections are the slowest of all (2 longer episodes), so they get the largest factor (2.0), and DAgger chunk is rescaled per-chunk depending on whether its anchor frame was human correction or an autonomous segment. As my own teleoperation got faster over the week, the right factor drifted, which is why this is set per-source rather than globally. Figure 10: Real-robot episodes per garment type, split by source (organizer BC, home teleop, home DAgger). 9.7 Aligning the environments The alignment half of 9.2 was mostly simple engineering: get the cameras, calibration, units, and motion to match the target as closely as could. The single most useful tool here was camera-overlay alignment utility (Figure 13). It picks frame from the organizer BC dataset, drives the physical robot to that exact joint state, captures the live cameras, and overlays them on the dataset frame. When the placement is right the robot positions line up. The same idea works for overlaying sim render against real frame (Figure 14). It made matching the physical rig to the source dataset quick visual loop instead of guesswork. It turned out useful well beyond my own setup: shared it with the organizers and other teams to help everyone align their evaluation environments at the competition. Ideal alignment was very hard. used different cameras, so it was never possible to overlay the images perfectly. Even during the final rollouts it seemed impossible to reproduce the evaluation setup exactly, even on the same hardware. also deliberately did the opposite of alignment where it helped robustness: randomized my own rig over time moving cameras, re-calibrating the arms several times, changing the lighting so the model would not overfit to any one configuration. This is the diversity lever applied to the data-collection rig itself. Figure 11: The four garment types in the organizers real-robot BC dataset, seen from the overhead camera. These are the physical garments the real policy folds the same four types shown in their original simulation form in 1. 9.10 DAgger collection and weighting Recovery data is the one thing the organizer BC set cannot provide it only ever shows clean, successful folds. To get off-distribution recovery behavior ran DAgger on the real robot: the policy folds autonomously, and the moment it starts to fail take over with teleop, correct it, and hand control back. Each frame is labeled by who was driving (policy vs. human). had no reward or value function on the real robot, so could not compute advantages the way the sim pipeline does. But the intervention signal is itself informative: human takeover marks recent failure of the policy, and the humans correction is by construction high-advantage action. turned that into per-frame sampling weights (Figure 17): Human-correction frames get the highest weight these are the demonstrations most want the model to learn. Autonomous frames far from any intervention get low weight (they are mostly the policy doing fine on its own little new signal). Autonomous frames in the 5-second window just before takeover ramp down to zero: specifically do not want to reinforce the exact moves that led the policy into the bad state. This is deliberately crude replacement for real advantage function. proper learned reward / value model on the real data would almost certainly help here both to weight frames more finely and to enable the kind of advantage conditioning and best-of-N selection that worked so well in sim but simply did not have time to build and validate one in the final week. The collection rig. To make DAgger ergonomic built setup with two leader arms and two follower arms, three-pedal foot controller to switch quickly between control modes, everything driven from single workstation with an RTX PRO 6000. For convenient handovers, the leader arms also track the followers while the policy is driving autonomously, so can grab control mid-motion without jump. focused DAgger collection on the hardest cases and outliers the hardest initial states where the policy tended to fail (but not too hard, the competition rules assumed flat initial garment state). In hindsight this may have been mistake: the actual final-round initial states were mostly easier than expected, so effort spent on hard-state recovery was partly wasted. suspect would have scored higher had instead concentrated on polishing clean completions of easy initial states. 20 Figure 12: The three cameras (top, left wrist, right wrist) for each training bucket: the organizer BC data, my own teleop/DAgger recordings, and the sim success-replays. Figure 13: The three live robot cameras overlaid on the matching organizer-BC frame after driving the arms to the recorded joint state. Figure 14: The same idea across the sim/real boundary: an Isaac Sim render of the same action sequence overlaid on the real cameras, used to tune the sims camera offsets against the real rig. 9.11 The units bug (a cautionary tale) lost lot of time to subtle data bug. Between two LeRobot 0.4.x releases the default state representation for the SO-101 follower changed the five arm joints switched from normalized 100 . . . 100 range-of-motion encoding to joint angles in degrees (the gripper is 0100 range either way). 21 Figure 15: The same real camera frame under independent draws of the augmentation stack color, gain, gamma, blur, noise, crop/rotate/zoom, and cutout. Every training frame is perturbed this way so the model cannot rely on any fixed appearance. match is hard to catch visually, yet it skews every joint by roughly 10%. Most of my real data and training ran on the wrong representation before finally tracked it down only two days before the deadline. fixed it with post-processing pass rather than re-collecting everything, but the disruption almost certainly cost some final performance. (The codebase has since been cleaned up so that real data is degree-mode end-to-end with no unit conversion anywhere on the training or inference path.) Figure 16: Per-source speed factors. 9.12 Final-round results placed 2nd in the final sim-to-real round. The top of the leaderboard, showing the teams that completed scored realrobot runs, is in Table 7: Figure 17: Per-frame sampling weight across an episode with two human interventions and return to autonomous control. Humancorrection frames get the highest weight, autonomous frames far from an intervention sit at the lower weight, and the 5-second windows just before each takeover ramp to zero. Rank Team 1 2 3 4 5 6 sZs ilya (this work) Dum-E SCUT-Unlimited sisigakgak Shubham @ Vorwerk Score 895 865 762.5 635 570 470 Table 7: Top of the final sim-to-real leaderboard. Score is the organizers composite real-robot metric, not success-rate percentage. Both success rate and per-step quality were evaluated, unseen garments had 50% score bonus, and the maximum possible total was 1080 points. The two conventions are related by per-joint rescale, and for most of the joints the full range of motion is around 200 degrees, so they look almost identical on screen and the misThe real-robot checkpoint behind this result is released at huggingface.co/IliaLarchenko/lehome_real.

sections.title: 10 Discussion
sections.content: The system that won the online round and came close second in sim-to-real wasnt one clean idea its pile of practical choices made under time pressure, most of them never properly ablated. Here is what think actually helped, what gave me trouble, and what would do next. 10.1 What would keep The policy as its own value function (5.1, 6.2). The success head turns the VLA into value estimator no separate critic to train, sync, or serve and the same forward pass also gives the completion, keypoint, and success signals that drive advantages and best-of-N. There is still lot of room to make these predictions more stable and better regularized, and this approach can have some downsides, so the trade offs should be assessed on case-by-case basis. Reward engineering (6). Finding the right reward for given task is still an art. Ground-truth checkpoints, success probability, completion, relative success each has trade-offs, and the trick is combining them well. My particular mix is surely far from optimal, but think these building blocks are the right ones. Throughput engineering (3.1). Data collection, manual or automatic, is numbers game: you have to squeeze every second. Parallelization, async failure-state generation, and anything else that saves operator time or yields more rollouts is worth it. Inference-time tuning (7). The same checkpoint can be optimized after training by tuning execution length, guidance scale, candidate count, and the rest found online with cheap Thompson-sampling bandit was big win that cost no extra training. DAgger data collection. Already standard, but worth repeating: collecting data from short human interventions is an efficient way to make policy more robust. Whenever possible, collect in DAgger style rather than full manual teleop, and keep interventions short. Unseen generalization was barely an issue. worried about the private unseen garments, but performance on them was only slightly below the trained ones, and the few misses were on garments genuinely far from anything in training. The sharpest case: real long-pants instance that looked like shorts even to me but officially needed the long-pants fold; in one of two rollouts the policy called it short pants and stopped early more labeling ambiguity than real failure. 10.2 What was hard or didnt work well Recovery in simulation. Teleoperating cloth through slow sim interface is hard, and by the time the DAgger tooling worked the policy folded better than could. Replays and augmentation made it more robust but not better at recovering state already wrecked see 10.4. Overfitting to rendering artifacts. Training only on sim, the model latched onto fine rendering and encoding details that dont transfer. It was not problem for the sim round but required lot of augmentation in the real one. never found clean fix beyond aligning the datacollection and evaluation environments, and the same overfitting also makes sim-to-real harder. The model is bigger than the task needs. used fairly large π0.5-based policy, but think this task could be solved with much smaller model. My main reason for entering was to experiment with full-scale VLA fine-tuning under RL, and that drove the model choice; in more practical setting would definitely start with something much smaller. 10.3 Robustness to environment changes One thing didnt expect was how well the policy held up to changes in the physical setup. BC policies especially on cheap hardware are usually fragile: nudge camera centimeter, change the focal length, shift the table, and the policy falls apart. Mine handled that kind of day-to-day drift relatively well. credit the deliberate rig randomization (9.7) and heavy augmentation (9.8). varied camera pose, lighting, color, and robot-base position on purpose, so no exact geometry was ever load-bearing, while the camera-overlay alignment kept that varied distribution centered on the real rig. More broadly, think cheap image augmentations are underused in robotics. They are standard in computer vision and transfer almost directly to robot camera streams, yet the field spends lot of effort on heavy neural-network image and video augmentation while the millisecond-cost classics is often unused. That has puzzled me for while. 10.4 Exploration and recovery: the open problem The hardest unsolved piece is automatic exploration. The proposed RL approach is great at reproducing and sharpening behavior it has seen, but it doesnt naturally explore its way out of states off the training distribution. Every inferencetime trick added pushes toward known-good modes, not new recovery: advantage conditioning and CFG amplify the average good behavior, best-of-N picks the least-bad of several samples from the same prefix, soft inpainting keeps the policy in the mode it already chose. None of them invent recovery move that isnt already in the data. experimented with few exploration mechanisms (some traces of which are still in the code), but none of them worked well: on flow-matching policy the extra perturbations mostly just push the chunk off the action manifold (2.2) and degrade performance, rather than turning up useful new recovery move. In practice the only reliable source of recovery was human intervention and it worked on the real robot but not in sim. few seconds of real teleop correction was easy and became one of the most useful tools in the project (9.10). So recovery split across the two rounds: sim relied on RL and replays for robustness, real relied on DAgger for recovery. Getting 23 flow-matching policy to generate useful recovery attempts without human in the loop still feels like an open problem. lation for Robotics. arXiv:2606.03551, 2026. https: //arxiv.org/abs/2606.03551 10.5 One pipeline instead of two My biggest regret is solving the two rounds with two mostly separate toolkits. The online round had the full RL machinery dense reward, value function, advantages, CFG, best-of-N. The sim-to-real round had real data and human interventions that actually produced recovery, but ran as plain BC because there was no real-side reward or value (9.5). These halves are complementary. single pipeline real-side reward/value function driving advantage conditioning and best-of-N on the real robot, prioritized sampling over real rollouts, and DAgger interventions weighted by that same value signal instead of the crude hand-tuned scheme used (9.10) would combine round 1s cleanliness with round 2s recovery. Id also feed it more clean completions from easy starting states rather than chasing hard cases, which over-indexed on (9.10). didnt have time to build this, but think it gets well past where either round landed alone my guess is 90%+ is achievable on this task. 10.6 Code and models checkpoints training, collection, and inference stack is The full github.com/IliaLarchenko/lehome_solution. at released the Hugging two final The the simulation-round policy at huggingFace Hub: face.co/IliaLarchenko/lehome_sim and the real-world-final policy at huggingface.co/IliaLarchenko/lehome_real. companion blog post with video examples is available at ilialarchenko.com/projects/lehome2026. are on
